questions.text: A point charge $+q$ is located at the center of a conducting uncharged spherical shell. Outside the shell is a point charge $+Q$. Then:
options: each of the two charges experience the same net (non-zero) force directed away from each other
there is no net force on $+q$ but a net force on $+Q$
each of the two charges experience zero net force
there is no net force on $+Q$ but a net force on $+q$

questions.text: Consider a uniformly charged hemisphere of radius $R$ charged uniformly with a volume density $\rho$. Consider points A and B both at a distance $2R$ from the center, as shown. If the electric field at A is denoted by $E$, then the electric field at B would be:
Experimenter A uses a test charge $q_0$ and experimenter B uses a test charge $-2q_0$ to measure an electric field produced by stationary charges. A finds a field that is:
options: less in magnitude than the field found by B
the same in both magnitude and direction as the field found by B
greater in magnitude than the field found by B
opposite in direction to the field found by B

questions.text: A ring of radius $R$ is uniformly charged with a charge $q$. A second coaxial ring of radius $r \ll R$, with its plane parallel to the first one, is moved at a constant speed along the axis. Which of the following graphs represent correctly the variation of the magnitude of the flux through the smaller ring as a function of time?

A potential difference is applied between a conducting sphere and a non-conducting plate ('plus' on the sphere and 'minus' on the plate). The dimensions of the plate are much larger than the distance between the sphere and the plate. A point positive charge is moved from point 1 to point 2 parallel to the plate. In this process, the work done by the external agent is:

Consider a layer of ideal dipoles lying in the xy-plane. Let the dipole moment per unit area be uniform and given by $\vec{\tau} = \tau\hat{k}$. Due to symmetry the potential will be a function of $z$ only. The difference in potential just above and just below the origin will be:
Choose the correct statement:
options: the potential of a negatively charged conductor must be negative
if $\vec{E}=0$ at a point P, then $V$ must be zero at P
if $V=0$ at a point P, then $\vec{E}$ must be zero at P
none of the above is correct

questions.text: An electron goes from one equipotential surface to another along one of the four paths shown below. Let $W_i$ denote the work done by the electric field on the electron for the i-th path. Then:

Two conducting spheres, one having twice the diameter of the other, are separated by a distance large compared to their diameters. The smaller sphere is charged while the larger one is neutral. They are now connected via a conducting wire. The fraction of the initial energy of the spheres lost as heat in the connecting wires is:
A long, hollow conducting cylinder is kept coaxially inside another long, hollow conducting cylinder of larger radius. Both the cylinders are initially electrically neutral.
options: A potential difference appears between the two cylinders when a charge density is given to the inner cylinder.
No potential difference appears between the two cylinders when a uniform line charge is kept along the axis of the cylinders.
A potential difference appears between the two cylinders when a charge density is given to the outer cylinder.
No potential difference appears between the two cylinders when same charge density is given to both the cylinders.

questions.text: Consider a cube which is filled uniformly with charge. The ratio of the potential at the center to that at a corner is:
Two uniform infinite sheets of electric charge densities $+\sigma$ and $-\sigma$ intersect at right angles. The magnitude of the electric field at an arbitrary point not on the sheets will be:
A conducting cubical shell has five of its faces grounded. The sixth face insulated from the rest is kept at a potential $V_0$. Then the potential at the center of the cube will be:

Two charged balls are initially moving with the same velocity. A uniform electric field is applied for a certain amount of time and removed. As a result, the direction of the velocity of the first ball changes by $60^{\circ}$, its speed being reduced by half, while the direction of the velocity of the second ball changes by $90^{\circ}$. If the charge-to-mass ratio of the first ball is $\gamma_1$ and that of the second one $\gamma_2$, then (neglecting the electrostatic interaction between the balls) the ratio $\gamma_1/\gamma_2$ equals:

Positive charge $Q$ is distributed uniformly throughout an insulating sphere of radius $R$, centred at the origin. A particle with positive charge $Q$ is placed at $x=2R$ on the axis. The magnitude of the electric field at $x=R/2$ on the z axis is:

A point charge $+Q$ is fixed at the point $(0, h)$ of the xy-plane. Another point charge $q$ moving on the xy plane only under the influence of the force due to $+Q$ goes from $A(-2h,h)$ to $B(2h,h)$ along the curve $x^2=4hy$. As $q$ passes through the origin its velocity is $v_0 \hat{i}$. Then the time taken by $q$ to go from A to B, is:

Consider a cylinder of length $l$ and diameter $d$, where $l \gg d$. It is charged uniformly on the surface. For points very close to the curved surface far from the ends, the electric field has a magnitude $E_0$. Then the field at a point on the axis whose distance from the center $r \gg l$ can be written down as:

In the situation represented in the drawing below, the two equipotential surfaces are infinite planes, perpendicular to the plane of the figure.

Consider the following statements:
S-1: The electric field in the conducting region and the insulating region are equal.
and
S-2: The electrostatic field inside a conductor is zero.
options: S-1 is true, S-2 is true; S-2 is a correct explanation for S-1
S-1 is true, S-2 is true; S-2 is NOT a correct explanation for S-1
S-1 is true, S-2 is false
S-1 is false, S-2 is true

questions.text: The self energy of thin uniformly charged insulating rod is given by $U=k\lambda^m l^n$ where $k$ is a constant, $\lambda$ the linear charge density, $l$ the length of the rod, and $m, n$ are positive real numbers greater than 1. When two rods, each of length $l$ and carrying equal linear charge density $\lambda$ are brought from infinity and placed together so as to coincide together, the work required is $W_1$. When the same two rods, being brought in from infinity, are placed end to end, the work required is $W_2$. Then $W_1/W_2$ is:
An electron moves along a metal tube with a variable cross section (see figure). As it approaches the thin neck of the tube:
options: its speed as well as the potential energy of electron + tube system increases
its speed decreases while the potential energy of electron + tube system increases
its speed as well as the potential energy of electron + tube system decreases
its speed increases while the potential energy of electron + tube system decreases

questions.text: A thin ring of radius $R$ carries a charge $q$ distributed on it such that the linear charge density increases linearly with the angle, for $0 \le \varphi \le 2\pi$. A neutral conducting sphere, also of radius $R$, is kept on the axis of the ring such that their centres are separated by a distance $\sqrt{3}R$, as shown. Let $k=1/4\pi\epsilon_0$. The potential at the point P is:

An arbitrary shaped neutral conductor has an arbitrary shaped cavity inside it. Inside the cavity are kept two equal and opposite charges.

S-1: There is no charge at all on the inner surface of the cavity.
and
S-2: Any extraneous charge given to a conductor reside on the outer surface of the conductor under the state of electrostatic equilibrium.
options: S-1 is true, S-2 is true; S-2 is a correct explanation for S-1
S-1 is true, S-2 is true; S-2 is NOT a correct explanation for S-1
S-1 is true, S-2 is false
S-1 is false, S-2 is true

questions.text: Two particle with charges $Q$ and $Q$ are fixed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle with sides of length $a$. If $k=1/4\pi\epsilon_0$, the work required to move a particle with charge $q$ from the other vertex to the center of the line joining the fixed particles is:

Three point charges are located at the vertices of an equilateral triangle. Two of them are identical with equal magnitude $Q$ while the third is opposite in sign. The magnitude of the third charge, so that the total interaction potential energy of the system is zero, is:

A point charge $q$ is placed at the origin O of the xy plane, while another point charge $Q$ is at the point $(d, 0)$. The variation of the x component, $E_x$, of the electric field along the axis between the charges is as shown below.

Which of the graphs below represents best the variation of the potential along the axis between the charges?

Two thin spherical shells, one with radius $R$ and the other with radius $2R$, surround an isolated charged point particle. The ratio of the number of field lines through the larger sphere to the number through the smaller is:

An electric dipole is positioned between a positive point charge and an infinitely large sheet charged with a uniformly distributed negative charge. The dipole is aligned parallel to the perpendicular dropped from the point charge to the sheet and its direction is towards the sheet. Once released:
options: the dipole will move towards the sheet
the dipole will neither move nor rotate
the dipole will move towards the point charge
the dipole will not move but will rotate about its center

questions.text: Two charged point particles are located at two vertices of an equilateral triangle and the electric field is zero at the third vertex. We conclude:
options: the two particles have charges with opposite signs and the same magnitude
the two particles have charges with opposite signs and different magnitudes
the two particles have identical charges

questions.text:
Consider a closed surface S enclosing a charge $q_1$. (The dashed line in the adjoining diagram shows the intersection of S with the plane of the figure.) There is another charge $q_2$ outside the surface. Let $\vec{E}_1$ and $\vec{E}_2$ be the electric fields generated by charges $q_1$ and $q_2$ respectively.

Then which of the following is true?

An electron moves from point i to point f, in the direction of a uniform electric field. For this displacement, let $W$ denote the work done by the field on the electron and let $U$ denote the potential energy of the 'electron-field' system. Then:

Figure (a) below shows three plastic sheets that are large, parallel, and uniformly charged. Figure (b) gives the component of the net electric field along an $x$-axis through the sheets. The scale of the vertical axis is set by $E_s = 6.0 \times 10^5$ N/C. What is the magnitude of the ratio of the charge density on sheet 3 to that on sheet 2?

The electrostatic potential for a spherically symmetric charge distribution varies with the radius $r$ as $V=ar^3+b$ where $a$ and $b$ are constants. The total charge in the sphere of unit radius is $\beta \pi a \epsilon_0$. The value of $\beta$ is
1728 small identical drops of mercury having the same charge on each, coalesce to form a bigger drop. Then the ratio of the potentials of the bigger drop to that of one of the initial smaller drop is
